semanticCore: Turning back, retreating on heels, recoil, escape, or fail

keyDerivations.meaning: To retreat, recoil on one's heels, turn back in fear, run away, or double back

figurativeOrRareUsages: فَلَمَّا تَرَاءَتِ الْفِئَتَانِ نَكَصَ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ وَقَالَ إِنِّي بَرِيءٌ مِنْكُمْ (8:48): But when the two forces came in sight of each other, he (Satan) recoiled/turned back on his heels and said: 'Indeed, I am disassociated from you' (Satan fleeing Badr).
summaryEn: The root represents recoiling and retreating (Nokoos), detailing Satan's sudden flight and retreat on his heels at Badr, and pagans turning back from listening to verses.
summaryAr: يمثل هذا الجذر الرجوع القهقري للخلف والارتداد السريع فزعاً (نكص)، مصوراً فرار الشيطان متراجعاً على عقبيه ببدر، وإعراض الكفار عن الآيات.
rawText:
إِنْتَكَصَ 8 see above. حَظُّهُ نَاقِصٌ وَجَدُّهُ نَاكصٌ ‡ [His lot, or portion, is decreasing, or deficient, and his good fortune is receding]. (A, TA.) مَنْكَصٌ , (O, B, K,) like مَقْعَدٌ, (TA,) [in the CK, erroneously, مُنَكَّص,] A place to which one removes, withdraws, or retires afar off, syn. مُتَنَحًّى. (O, B, K.) El-Aashà says, praising 'Alkamah Ibn-'Olátheh,

أَعَلْقَمُ قَدْ جَبَرَتْنِى الأُمُورُ

إِلَيْكَ وَمَا كَانَ لِى مَنْكَصُ

[O 'Alkamah, affairs have compelled me to have recourse to thee, and there was not for me any place to which to retire. (TA.) نَكَصَ عَنِ الأَمْرِ 1 , (A, K,) [aor. ﹻ, and sometimes ﹹ, as will be shown below,] inf. n. نُكُوصٌ (S, IF, A, Msb, K) and نَكُصٌ (A, K) and مَنْكَصٌ (K) and نَكَصَانٌ, (MA,) He receded, retired, or drew back from the thing, or affair, refrained, forbore, abstained, or desisted, from it; recoiled from it, shrank from it, or drew back from it in awe or fear; (S, IF, A, Msb, K;) as also نَكَثَ عَنْهُ. (Aboo-Turáb, TA.) You say also, نَكَصَ عَلَى عَقِبَيْهِ, (S, Msb, K,) or على عَقِبِهِ, (A,) aor. ﹹ (S, Msb) and ﹻ, (S,) or the latter only, (Sgh, TA,) or the former is allowable, (Zj, TA,) He turned back from a thing to which he had applied himself; (IDrd, S, * A, * Msb, * K, TA;) meaning a good thing; and sometimes an evil thing; (IDrd, TA;) or meaning particularly a good thing; or extr. with respect to an evil thing. (K.) [See the Kur, xxiii. 68.] Hence 🡻انتكص [as syn. with نكص], though we have not heard it. (Mgh.)
